## ***OBJECTIVE OF THE DELIVERABLE 0:*** {#objective-of-the-deliverable-0:}

- The main objective is to define the challenge under consideration and come up with a comprehensive work plan for the project. These are an understanding of the challenge, description of the training partner, and the Project Charter.

## ***UNDERSTANDING THE CHALLENGE AND TRAINING PARTNER:*** {#understanding-the-challenge-and-training-partner:}

1. **Description of the challenge we are addressing:**

   The challenge lies in assessing and developing the planning and control processes at NextGen Deliveries, a last mile logistics Amazon operation in Miami, Florida. Our proposed approach targets at assessing the organization's system of performance indicators to identify gaps and areas for improvement. During this project we will do the following activities:

   1) Diagnosis of current indicator systems:  
      * Evaluating the suitability of existing performance in order to evaluate competitiveness of the company.  
        * Evaluating the existing paradigm and finding problems that arise with types and structures of presentation of indicators, as well as with their visualization.  
        2) Development of a metrics dashboard:  
           * Developing a concept of an FC that improves the visual and operational indicators analysis through exceptional responding interface.  
           * Making certain that the dashboard factor has future data analysis functionality that would prove useful in the decision-making process.  
        3) Proposals for improvement:  
           * Developing specific organizational strategies for increasing the competitiveness from the given analysis of the performance indicators.  
           * These have to be considered as priorities with a view to the ways in which they can be structured more effectively.  
   2. Overview of the training partner's role in this project:

   **NextGen Deliveries:**

      A prominent last-mile logistics company based in Miami, FL, specializing in delivering packages for Amazon. As a key player in the logistics sector, NextGen Deliveries plays an important role in ensuring timely and efficient delivery of goods to customers. The company’s operations are characterized by:

      1) Stressing on efficient systems and shortened turnaround time possible to fulfill customers’ expectations.   
         2)  The applicability of better technologies in logistics that would enable one to navigate the delivery routes and processes better.   
         3)  Restructuring of the service delivery by adopting several key performance indicators as ways of measuring improved operational effectiveness.   
         4)  Using statistical analysis as the primary method to making operational decisions and enhancing customers’ satisfaction.

# 

| DELIVERABLE 1 |
| :---: |

## ***Introduction***

### **Background**

The packaging industry plays a vital role in global supply chains, enabling the safe transportation, storage, and marketing of products across diverse sectors such as food and beverage, pharmaceuticals, and e-commerce. Over the years, the industry has seen a significant transformation with a growing focus on sustainability, lightweight materials, and innovative packaging designs that minimize waste. As consumer expectations for eco-friendly packaging rise, companies are increasingly adopting biodegradable, recyclable, or reusable packaging solutions. Packaging not only serves practical purposes but also plays a crucial role in brand identity, offering businesses a way to differentiate themselves in a competitive market.

**Amazon**, as a global leader in e-commerce, has been a key player in shaping packaging trends. Given the immense volume of orders it handles, the company has committed to more sustainable practices through its Frustration-Free Packaging initiative and partnerships with companies to reduce waste and environmental impact. **NextGEN**, an emerging player in packaging innovation, focuses on developing forward-thinking solutions for companies like Amazon, emphasizing sustainability, cost efficiency, and technological advancements in packaging design. NextGEN’s mission aligns with the industry’s shift towards a circular economy, leveraging cutting-edge technology to improve packaging operations while reducing its ecological footprint.

### **Diagnosis of Performance Indicators.**

**Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)** are essential tools that businesses use to measure performance, efficiency, and competitiveness within specific parts of their operations. For example, **Amazon**, one of the leading e-commerce companies, focuses on logistics and fulfillment operations by tracking KPIs such as order fulfillment time, inventory turnover, on-time delivery rate, and warehouse utilization rate to ensure high operational efficiency and customer satisfaction. Similarly, **Tesla** measures its production and innovation by tracking production rate, battery efficiency, R\&D investment as a percentage of revenue, and CO2 emissions per vehicle, which are crucial to maintaining its position in the electric car and clean energy markets.

In retail, **Walmart** monitors sales per square foot, stockout rate, gross profit margin, and customer satisfaction score (CSAT) to gauge retail efficiency and customer experience. **Apple**, known for its innovative products, uses KPIs like time to market, customer retention rate, market share, and gross margin to stay competitive and ensure product profitability. Meanwhile, **Toyota**, renowned for its lean manufacturing processes, tracks defect rate, cycle time, first-pass yield (FPY), and energy usage per vehicle to maintain quality, efficiency, and sustainability in its automotive production. These KPIs, tailored to specific industry requirements, enable companies to optimize their processes continuously, respond to market demands, and maintain a competitive edge.

### **Systematic View of the Current Situation**

Through the development of a background investigation of the package delivery and Last Mile industry in the USA, the project was able to retrieve a high level overview of the area of concern for NextGEN. Secondly, through the kickoff and consultation sessions, the project was able to dive deeper into the areas of influence and control of both the Amazon Delivery Service Partner Program and the company’s department.   
![][image4]  
***Figure 1\.*** Systemic Map of NextGEN Current Situation  
Figure 1 summarizes the background research of the project. Firstly, the Area of Concern (and the out-of-scope elements) include stakeholders such as Amazon, the Miami community, and Government, that either directly or indirectly control elements of concern to NextGEN, such as both Amazon and Last Mile Logistic industries, customer demand, workforce, and industry competitors. Secondly, the Area of Influence includes all of the Amazon Delivery Service Partner Program’s (ADSP) systems that influence the operations and profitability of NextGEN. It includes systems of Route Targets Forecasting and Amazon DSP Scorecard –which determine the performance incentives given by the program. Thirdly, the Area of Control focuses on the operative dimension of the company, which oversees the route-driver planning, determined by both the Amazon Scorecard Metrics and NextGEN Metrics. Finally, it is crucial to note that the systematic map determines the internal elements that are of importance for the project objective –to assess the KPI’s in order to enhance driver ranking and performance evaluation– and how these systems aid to achieve a goal of profitability improvement. 

## ***Development***

### **Balanced Scorecard**

Based upon the background research of the project, and the insight of the macro and micro processes that have an effect upon the project, a balanced scorecard was developed. Figure 2 is also found in Annex 2\.   
![][image5]  
***Figure 2\.*** Balanced Scorecard

After analyzing the mission, vision, and industry’s/stakeholder’s requirements for the company, Figure 2 conceptualizes the strategic objectives, metrics, goals, and initiatives in each one of the BSC’s perspectives. 

Firstly, the Financial Perspective oversees the key financial objectives that NextGEN must achieve to satisfy its shareholders. It controls both revenue, expenses and profit of the company. Secondly, the Customer Perspective is based upon the client (Amazon) and customer needs, such as Delivery Completion Rate, Standard Work Compliance, and Customer Delivery Experience –performance indicators derived from the DSP Scorecard system. Third, the Internal Processes Perspective is concerned with what NextGEN must do well in order to meet its performance objectives. You can see how this viewpoint would have a high importance: Keeping the tickets down reduces breaking your own policies and some real hazards for your drivers, safety is an all or nothing kind of deal after all. NextGEN intends to make themselves a safety-first, consistent last-mile delivery service for both Amazon and the neighborhood by focusing on these internal metrics. Finally, the Learning and Growth Perspective says that you need to go out of your way to train and keep your employees around. This perspective is also in line with the company's objective of developing a high-performance culture by growing the proportion of top performing drivers and shrinking low performing drivers. Programs like mentorship, ongoing training and focused support for low performers are structured to increase overall team competence. In addition, career pathing and recognition strategies help retain employees by keeping institutional knowledge within the company and fostering employee satisfaction, thus contributing to the future success of a business.

These four NextGEN performance drivers are the basis of a complete approach to driving NextGEN's performance that maintains alignment with \- (1) strategic goals and mission impact, (2), stakeholder expectations; and (3) operational efficiency or cost reduction—all components of NextGen's ability to perform their core service offerings. The three perspectives are linked and together they characterize a path for the company to track progress from multiple dimensions while simultaneously improving overall performance in order to achieve long-term success. This was an import analysis to now proceed with the Strategic Map.

### **Strategic Map**

![][image6]  
***Figure 3\.*** Strategic Map

The strategic map presented in Figure 3 visually illustrates the relationships between the key objectives established for this project. It is structured across four rows, each representing a critical perspective. The Financial Perspective reflects the company’s primary economic goals, focusing on profitability and cost management. The Customer Perspective captures the company’s aim to enhance customer satisfaction and loyalty through improved service delivery. The Internal Process Perspective highlights the need for operational improvements and efficiency in day to day tasks, while the Learning and Growth Perspective emphasizes employee development, training and overall organizational growth.

On the other axis, we have the columns, which represent the strategic themes. The first theme, Operational Efficiency, focuses on optimizing the whole process to reduce waste and improve productivity; next is Quality, which ensures that the services meet high standards consistently. Customer Centric Deliveries emphasize delivering services that align with the customer needs and expectations, ensuring satisfaction at every delivery. Lastly, Employee Performance reflects the company’s commitment to fostering a culture of high performance, employee growth and engagement with them.

The diagram integrates all objectives, demonstrating how they align with the key focus areas and strategic themes. It also highlights the interconnectedness of the objectives, showing how improvements in one area can drive progress in others. This holistic view ensures that every objective contributes to the broader strategic goals of the company, ensuring a thorough approach to success.

### **Definition of Objectives and Indicators.**

To ensure alignment between the company’s strategic goals and the previous Balanced Scorecard, the following objectives, sub-objectives. and KPIs (Key Performance Indicators) have been developed using the SMART criteria (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound). These objectives are linked to the financial, customer, internal processes, and learning and growth perspectives in order to provide clear metrics to track success.

1. **FINANCIAL PERSPECTIVE:**  
   1. Objective 1.1: Increase revenue during the next fiscal period to enhance operational profitability.  
      1. Sub-objective 1.1.1: Achieve a Fantastic or Fantastic Plus standing on the DSP Scorecard to qualify for Amazon Performance Incentives.  
      2. *KPIs:*  
         1. Revenue Growth Rate (Target: X%)  
         2. DSP Scorecard Overall Standing (Target: Fantastic or higher)  
   2. Objective 1.2: Reduce labor costs to improve profitability.  
      1. Sub-objective 1.2.1: Reduce overtime hours by assigning demanding routes to more efficient drivers.  
      2. *KPIs:*  
         1. Overtime Hours Reduction (Target: X%)  
         2. Labor Cost per Route (Target: X% decrease)  
   3. Objective 1.3: Increase profit to support business growth and shareholder value.  
      1. Sub-objective 1.3.1: Achieve cost efficiency by improving route assignment and optimizing driver productivity.  
      2. *KPIs:*  
         1. Profit Margin (Target: X%)  
         2. Operational Expense Reduction (Target: X%)

2. **CUSTOMER PERSPECTIVE:**  
   1. Objective 2.1: Improve delivery completion rate to reduce returns and enhance customer satisfaction.  
      1. Sub-objective 2.1.1: Provide continuous driver training and feedback.  
      2. *KPIs:*  
         1. Delivery Completion Rate (Target: X%)  
         2. Return Rate Reduction (Target: X%)  
   2. Objective 2.2: Increase standard work compliance to enhance service quality.  
      1. Sub-objective 2.2.1: Ensure drivers adhere to operational procedures through regular assessments.  
      2. *KPIs:*  
         1. Standard Work Compliance (Target: X%)  
         2. Customer Satisfaction Score (Target: X%)  
   3. Objective 2.3: Reduce negative customer delivery experiences to improve overall ratings.  
      1. Sub-objective 2.3.1: Gather direct feedback from customers and implement corrective actions.  
      2. KPIs:  
         1. Customer Delivery Experience (Target: X% decrease in negative feedback)  
         2. Customer Complaint Resolution Rate (Target: X%)

3. **INTERNAL PROCESSES PERSPECTIVE:**  
   1. Objective 3.1: Reduce traffic violation incidents to improve safety and compliance.  
      1. Sub-objective 3.1.1: Conduct driving safety seminars for drivers.  
      2. KPIs:  
         1. Traffic Violation Rate (Target: X% reduction)  
         2. Sign/Signal Violations (Target: X%)  
   2. Objective 3.2: Decrease speeding incidents to maintain a safe company image and reduce accidents.  
      1. Sub-objective 3.2.1: Install sound alerts to notify drivers when speed limits are exceeded.  
      2. KPIs:  
         1. Speeding Event Rate (Target: X%)  
         2. Accident Rate Reduction (Target: X%)  
   3. Objective 3.3: Increase seat belt usage to ensure driver safety.  
      1. Sub-objective 3.3.1: Monitor seatbelt usage and provide continuous driver improvement sessions.  
      2. KPIs:  
         1. Seat Belt Compliance Rate (Target: X%)  
         2. Seatbelt Off Rate Reduction (Target: X%)

4. **LEARNING AND GROWTH PERSPECTIVE:**  
   1. Objective 4.1: Foster a high-performance culture by improving driver performance.  
      1. Sub-objective 4.1.1: Implement mentorship programs and tailored training for high performers.  
      2. KPIs:  
         1. High Performers Share (Target: X%)  
         2. Training Completion Rate (Target: X%)  
   2. Objective 4.2: Reduce low performer rates to improve overall team performance.  
      1. Sub-objective 4.2.1: Identify root causes of underperformance and offer targeted support.  
      2. KPIs:  
         1. Low Performers Share (Target: X%)  
         2. Performance Improvement Rate (Target: X%)  
   3. Objective 4.3: Increase employee retention to strengthen institutional knowledge.  
      1. Sub-objective 4.3.1: Create a retention strategy with career development opportunities.  
      2. KPIs:  
         1. Employee Retention Rate (Target: X%)  
         2. Tenured Workforce Share (Target: X%)

      ### **Relationships Between Objectives**

On the Strategic Map (Figure 3), we can observe the  interconnectedness of the objectives and how they contribute to the company’s overarching goals. By exploring these relationships, we can understand how improvements in one area influence others, creating a holistic strategy that drives the company forward. The following explanation will detail how each objective aligns with one another:  
**Objective 1.1 (Increase revenue)** 

- **Objective 1.2 (Reduce labor costs to enhance profitability):** By reducing the costs of labor, the company can retain a larger portion of the revenue that they have, enhancing profitability as well. By optimizing the delivery process, with the most efficient drivers, the company will rescue labor costs of extra hours, affecting directly to the profitability and revenue of the company. When labor costs are managed efficiently, stable or increasing revenue can lead to significant improvements in profitability.

- **Objective 1.3 (Increase profit):** By increasing revenue, the company will simultaneously enhance its profit, as both objectives contribute to achieving its financial goals. Higher revenue provides more capital to cover expenses, thereby mitigating potential losses. This additional financial flexibility allows the company to invest strategically, reduce operational costs and ultimately increase overall profitability; focusing on revenue growth will create a cycle that strengthens financial health and supports long term business success.

- **Objective 2.1 (Improve delivery completion rates):** By enhancing operational efficiency and customer satisfaction, both drivers and the company can achieve improved performance metrics that indicate service quality. Higher performance rates reflect the effectiveness of delivery services, leading to a better customer experience. As delivery quality improves, the company is likely to see increased revenue from successful deliveries and greater customer loyalty. In the end, better performance enhances the company's reputation and drives financial growth through more positive delivery outcomes.  
    
- **Objective 2.2 (Increase standard work compliance):** Ensuring that drivers adhere to operational procedures enhances overall operational efficiency and establishes standardized work practices. This adherence minimizes errors and significantly improves customer satisfaction. As customer satisfaction increases, the company benefits from improved ratings and strengthened customer trust, leading to greater loyalty and repeat purchases. This cycle of trust and satisfaction ultimately drives revenue growth, as satisfied customers are more likely to continue using the company’s services.

- **Objective 2.3 (Reduce negative customer experiences):** By minimizing negative customer experiences, the company will foster growth driven by valuable feedback from its customers. Strong compliance with customer expectations will enhance the company’s overall reputation, positively impacting the quality of its services. This commitment to excellence will encourage ongoing customer engagement, ultimately leading to increased revenue.

- **Objective 4.2 (Improve overall team performance):** The company evaluates its performance through various metrics. By targeting and reducing negative or low rates, and by focusing on enhancing operational efficiency and customer satisfaction, the company can achieve higher performance ratings. This improvement not only boosts overall team performance—reflecting the company’s reputation—but also leads to better compliance with established standards. As performance rates rise, the company is positioned to generate increased revenue.

**Objective 1.2 (Reduce labor costs)**

- **Objective 1.3 (Increase profit):** By reducing labor costs, the company will simultaneously enhance its profits. Labor costs represent a significant expense, and by minimizing overtime pay for drivers, the company can reduce its overall expenditures. This reduction in costs directly contributes to improved profit margins, allowing the company to direct more resources toward growth and operational improvements.

- **Objective 2.1 (Improve delivery completion rates):** By enhancing delivery completion rates through continuous driver training, the company can significantly improve performance. As drivers become more skilled and efficient, the company’s overall ratings will rise. This improvement means fewer missed deliveries and, consequently, reduced need for overtime pay. Employing better-performing drivers, means the company can decrease labor costs associated with non-deliveries, leading to a more efficient operation and lower overall expenses.

- **Objective 2.2 (Increase standard work compliance):** When drivers adhere to standard work methods, it ensures they perform their tasks correctly and efficiently. This compliance allows them to complete all scheduled deliveries on time, reducing the need for overtime pay. As a result, the company can lower its labor costs, contributing to improved operational efficiency and profitability.

- **Objective 4.1 (High-performance culture):** The company must cultivate a high-performance culture, as drivers are assigned a specific number of deliveries within a designated time frame. Having this mindset and providing targeted training, drivers will become more effective in meeting their delivery goals. This focus on high performance ensures completion of deliveries in time, which reduces the overtime pay. This approach will lower labor costs and enhance profitability for the company.

**Objective 1.3 (Increase profit)**

- **Objective 2.1 (Improve delivery completion rates):** The company evaluates its performance through various metrics, and by enhancing delivery completion rates, it can significantly boost profitability. Improved training will enable drivers to perform their tasks more effectively, ensuring timely completion of all deliveries. This increased efficiency not only elevates the company’s overall performance but also leads to higher profits as a result of reduced operational costs and improved customer satisfaction.

- **Objective 2.2 (Increase standard work compliance):** By enhancing compliance with standard work procedures, drivers will have a clear framework for completing their deliveries, leading to increased efficiency and operational effectiveness. This, in turn, will improve the company’s ratings and elevate customer satisfaction, resulting in more repeat purchases; in consequence, these factors will contribute to increased profitability for the company.  
    
- **Objective 2.3 (Reduce negative customer experiences):** Achieving higher customer ratings indicates that deliveries are being completed accurately and on time, leading to an overall improvement in the company’s reputation; if the company is having lower ratings, they will in turn implement corrective measures to enhance performance. As previously mentioned, improved ratings not only boost customer satisfaction but also drive profitability for the company by increasing customer loyalty and repeat business.

- **Objective 4.1 (High-performance culture):** Instilling a culture of high performance within the company will foster driver development and encourage adherence to this methodology, leading to enhanced delivery performance. As drivers improve, the overall efficiency of the company will increase, elevating quality and customer satisfaction, which ultimately drives higher profits. Additionally, this culture will help the company reduce expenses due to the operational effectiveness and employee performance, such as overtime pay, further contributing to improved profitability.  
    
- **Objective 4.2 (Improve overall team performance):** When every team member performs their duties as expected, it signifies that the entire company is operating efficiently. This collective effectiveness will enhance the company's profitability by ensuring all areas function optimally, leading to increased productivity, reduced operational costs, and improved customer satisfaction. As a result, the company will experience higher sales and repeat business, ultimately contributing to greater profit margins.

- **Objective 4.3 (Increase employee retention):** Having loyal employees translates to long-term retention, ensuring they consistently meet job expectations. This loyalty minimizes the need for extensive training of new hires, saving the company both time and money—expenses that are critical to manage. Additionally, experienced employees enhance performance and productivity, facilitating smoother operations. This stability ultimately contributes to increased profitability for the organization, ensuring it operates effectively.

**Objective 2.1 (Improve delivery completion rates)**

- **Objective 2.2 (Increase standard work compliance):** By implementing standard work compliance, drivers will enhance their delivery performance by adhering to established guidelines that help them complete their tasks on time. This will lead to improved delivery efficiency and higher service quality, resulting in better performance ratings and completion rates. Focusing on standardizing company operations will significantly enhance the overall success of deliveries.

- **Objective 2.3 (Reduce negative customer experiences):** One of the key metrics for evaluating driver performance is customer satisfaction. If deliveries are made on time and in good condition, customer satisfaction will improve. As a result, higher customer delivery rates will reflect positively on both the driver and the company, leading to increased delivery completion rates.  
    
- **Objective 3.1 (Reduce traffic violation incidents):** Reducing traffic violations directly impacts delivery completion rates. When drivers avoid violations, they are less likely to be involved in accidents that can affect their progress. This means deliveries can be completed on time and as scheduled. In contrast, when violations occur, they may need rerouting or assigning a different driver, leading to delays and missed deadlines. So, minimizing traffic violations not only enhances driver safety but also ensures a more reliable delivery process, boosting overall completion rates.

- **Objective 4.1 (High-performance culture):** By fostering a culture that promotes high performance, drivers are motivated to work more efficiently and effectively, ensuring timely and accurate deliveries; leading to an increase in delivery completion rates, as drivers adhere to established standards and procedures. A high-performance culture not only improves operational efficiency but also encourages accountability among drivers, further solidifying the company's commitment to quality service.  
    
- **Objective 4.2 (Improve overall team performance):** Improving delivery completion rates indicates that both drivers and the company are operating effectively and efficiently. Each driver is a representative of the company and when they perform as expected, it reflects positively on the organization as a whole. This collective effort enhances the overall performance of the team and also contributes to the company’s reputation and customer satisfaction. 

- **Objective 4.3 (Increase employee retention):** Having employees who are already familiar with the company’s operations and procedures significantly boosts delivery completion rates. When the employees are kept motivated, inspired and helped to grow their careers within the organization, they maintain a high level of performance and commitment. This continuous engagement enhances the quality of service provided but also fosters a culture of excellence, contributing to improved delivery outcomes and overall success for the company.

**Objective 2.2 (Increase standard work compliance)**

- **Objective 2.3 (Reduce negative customer experiences):** By increasing standard work compliance, drivers will follow the established procedures, resulting in more reliable and efficient deliveries. When deliveries are done correctly and on time, customer satisfaction improves significantly, reducing the chances of errors or delays, which are often the causes of negative customer experiences, so in turn it will enhance the overall customer satisfaction.

- **Objective 3.1 (Reduce traffic violation incidents):** By implementing specific guidelines, drivers will be required to adhere to established instructions, ensuring they follow the correct procedures. Regular check-ups will help monitor compliance, allowing drivers to identify potential traffic rule violations before they occur. This proactive approach not only reduces incidents of traffic violations but also enhances overall standard work compliance, leading to safer and more efficient operations.

- **Objective 3.2 (Decrease speeding incidents):** Establishing clear protocols will require drivers to adhere to specific speed limits, having safer driving habits. By conducting regular evaluations, the company can monitor compliance, helping drivers recognize and modify any speeding tendencies. This focus on compliance will lead to a substantial reduction in speeding incidents, promoting a safer driving environment and enhancing overall operational effectiveness.

- **Objective 3.3 (Increase seat belt usage):** One of the established guidelines will encourage drivers to consistently wear seat belts while on the road. By integrating regular checks into their routines, they will be reminded of the importance of the usage of a seat belt and that they need to be safe. Compliance with this, will create a culture of safety and minimize the risk of injuries in any type of accident.

- **Objective 4.2 (Improve overall team performance):** Enhancing work standard compliance will ensure that drivers adhere to the established habits and protocols set by the company. By following this structured approach; knowing what tasks to perform, how to execute them, and when to complete them, drivers will boost their performance and also help increase the overall effectiveness of the company. This, in result, will lead to improved operational efficiency and increased performance metrics across the board.

**Objective 2.3 (Reduce negative customer experiences)**

- **Objective 4.2 (Improve overall team performance):** Improving customer satisfaction makes the entire company grow. When deliveries are made on time and without any issues, customers have less to complain about and experience a higher quality of service. This positive shift reflects not just the efforts of individual drivers but the collective performance of the whole team. If everyone works together to meet the customer needs, it starts to build trust and loyalty among them, benefiting the company as a whole.

**Objective 3.1 (Reduce traffic violation incidents)**

- **Objective 3.2 (Decrease speeding incidents):**  When drivers adhere to traffic laws and regulations, they significantly reduce the likelihood of speeding incidents. By maintaining appropriate speed limits, drivers not only avoid traffic violations but also minimize the risk of accidents. This is crucial for maintaining the company's reputation, as safe driving practices reflect positively on the organization and contribute to overall customer satisfaction.

- **Objective 3.3 (Increase seat belt usage):** Reducing traffic violations, including those related to seat belt use, is directly linked to increasing seat belt usage. By promoting a culture of safety that emphasizes compliance with traffic laws, drivers are more likely to use their seat belts, in turn, decreasing the risk of injury in case of any accident.  
    
- **Objective 4.2 (Improve overall team performance):** When there are fewer traffic violations, it creates a sense of discipline and accountability among drivers, which benefits not only as an individual driver but also the entire team's performance. This commitment to safety demonstrates that the company values and adheres to laws and regulations, prioritizing the well-being and security of its employees. By promoting a culture of responsibility, drivers are more likely to perform their jobs effectively, ensuring that deliveries are made safely and efficiently. 

- **Objective 4.3 (Increase employee retention):** By prioritizing the well-being of employees and working to prevent traffic incidents, the company demonstrates its commitment to their safety and health. This focus on creating a secure work environment not only protects employees but also promotes loyalty and trust. When drivers feel valued and cared for, they are more likely to stay with the company long term, contributing to a stable and experienced workforce. 

**Objective 3.2  (Decrease speeding incidents)**

- **Objective 4.2 (Improve overall team performance):** By decreasing speeding incidents, the company will see an improvement in overall performance, as drivers will be safer and more reliable. This reduction in accidents and violations will lead to better delivery rates, enhance customer satisfaction and prevent delays. This positively impacts each driver's reputation and also strengthens the company's reputation as a whole, promoting trust and reliability in their services.

- **Objective 4.3 (Increase employee retention):** By decreasing speeding incidents, the company ensures that employees are being taken care of and that they are adhering to the safety guidelines. This focus on employee well-being not only keeps them safe and healthy, but also gives them a sense of value and care within the organization. As a result, employee retention improves, as workers feel supported and secure in their roles, knowing their safety is a priority.


**Objective 3.3 (Increase seat belt usage):** 

- **Objective 4.2 (Improve overall team performance):** With the increase of the usage of seat belts, the drivers will be safer in their roles in the company, ensuring that they are fulfilling their roles as employees how they are supposed to, but while doing so, they are being kept safe. This will impact the overall team performance by having employees in good health and doing their jobs correctly, increasing the efficiency and reducing any possible risks.   
    
- **Objective 4.3 (Increase employee retention):** The use of seat belts is crucial for drivers to prevent serious health risks in the event of an accident; by emphasizing the importance of seat belt usage and providing clear safety guidelines, drivers will feel a sense of protection, knowing that the company prioritizes their well being. This not only increases their safety but also creates loyalty, as employees are more likely to stay with an organization that shows genuine care for them. Additionally, ensuring drivers are in good physical health allows them to perform their jobs effectively and safely.

**Objective 4.1 (High-performance culture)**

- **Objective 4.2 (Improve overall team performance):** Promoting a high-performance culture encourages employees to maximize their potential, leading to improved job performance. When employees consistently go for excellence, the quality and efficiency of the company's service improve, boosting overall team performance. This commitment to high standards reflects positively on the company, enhancing its reputation as a hardworking and reliable organization that gives quality results.

### **Selected Key Performance Indicators (KPIs).**

Based of analyzing the insights of the dataset it emphasizes on 3 main categories of the defined kpis on the delivery of the products by the truck drivers, which are Safety, Quality and Team performance, by reviewing the pre-existing performance metrics, the new integrated kpis that will improve the overall rating of the truck drivers, most of them expressed as a percentage to have an easier insight of what is taking place on the company.

**Safety** 

* Critical safety routes:   
  Measuring the amount of percentage of routes that involved a critical safety incident (e.g., accident or near-miss). With the formulation of:  
  (Critical Incidents/ Total of routes) \* 100  
  Making it easier to focus on the routes that have a higher probability of an incident based on the amount of incidents by route.  
* Driver fatigue risk rate:   
  Percentage of routes completed in which the driver was exceeding the predetermined limit of driving hour or routes without the sufficient amount of brakes. Measured with:  
  (Routes by fatigued drivers/ Routes) \* 100  
  The routes by fatigued drivers can be defined by the exceeding the set maximum of hours or consecutive routes without a rest.  
  The goal is to monitor and minimize the number of routes in which the drivers can be at any fatigue condition, reducing the amount of safety incidents caused by fatigue. 

**Quality**

* Failed delivery due to an incorrect address:   
  Percentage of any failed delivery due to an incomplete or incorrect description of the customer address. Calculated with:  
  (Failed delivery due to Address/ Total failed deliveries) \* 100  
  The objective of this kpi is to identify the amount of deliveries in which the delivery failure was caused by any address issue, in order to improve address verification and reduce idle times based on the driver exploring for the correct address.  
    
* Customer engagement:  
  Percentage of customers in which interacted with the tracking system provided or provided any feedback on the system. It can be easily measured with:  
  (Customer engagements/ Total deliveries) \* 100  
  Helping get a better insight on what are the factors to enhance a customer satisfaction and why some branches of customers don’t utilize any of the system of any delivery.

**Team**

* Driver performance consistency:  
  Percentage of drivers whose performance on the balance scorecard remains consistent across multiple weeks (At least 4 weeks). How can a driver be measured of consistency? By having minimal variance in their key metrics, formulated with:  
  (Drivers with consistent performance/ Total drivers) \* 100  
  The objective of this kpi is to identify which drivers are prone to fluctuations, useful for any improvement plans.


**Additional efficiency KPIS**

* Fuel waste reduction:  
  Focusing on reducing unnecessary fuel consumption, arising from either inefficient routes, traffic delays, poor driving or any excessive idling which can be easily represented by:  
  (Previous fuel waste \- Current fuel wastePrevious fuel waste) \* 100  
  In which drivers who frequently waste fuel could benefit from training on driving techniques, it is easier to correlate the optimal routes due to idle time regarding any high-traffic or stop-heavy, and also analyze the fuel waste based on the vehicle driven.  
* Route deviations:  
  Tracking the percentage decrease in the number of deviations of drivers on already planned routes, due to multiple factors. Utilizing the formula:  
  (Previous route deviations \- Current route deviationsPrevious route deviations) \* 100  
  By reducing these deviations, the operational efficiency increases and fuel consumption is optimized, reducing unnecessary mileage, by correlating the route deviations with fuel consumption patterns and how these leads to fuel inefficiencies it can be easier to be tracked, we can also have a better seasonality insight based off the hours in which the driver is delivering the specific packaging.

### **Initial Statistical Analysis**

#### **Descriptive statistics** 

![][image7]  
				***Table 1***. Safety descriptive 

While analyzing the standard deviation of the shown variables some of them are higher than 3, representing that data points are widely dispersed, with values deviating significantly from the mean so there is a wide variability in key safety metrics like Seatbelt, Speeding and Signal, meaning that while most drivers adhere to the safety requirements, there is a subset of drivers that frequently violates those safety protocols, high outliers, shown in figure 5 (Appendix F) are a significant area of concern that as an action plan, targeted training or monitoring should be assessed.

![][image8]  
				***Table 2***. Quality descriptive

Overall, the quality metrics highlight consistent patterns across certain metrics, such as DCR and POD, which show low variability, indicating that delivery completion rates and photo quality are close to their respective averages. However, DSB's high variability suggests inconsistency in drivers' adherence to best practices, with some drivers possibly struggling with delivery success behavior. Low variability in metrics like DCR and POD means results are consistently near the average, not necessarily that performance is strong or weak.           

* DCR: Nearly symmetric, low variability with values tightly clustered around the mean, consistently high delivery rate.  
* DSB: Right-skewed with high variability, indicating a wide spread and potential outliers on the high end so there are occasional delivery issues due to non-compliance.  
* POD: Symmetric with low variability, showing values close to the mean, high consistency.  
* CC: Slight left-skew, moderate spread around the mean, meaning mostly compliant contact attempts.  
* CDF: Symmetric with moderate variability, values broadly balanced across the customer feedback.

![][image9]  
				***Table 3***. Efficiency descriptive

Based on Hr/Rout one can observe that some drivers or routes are pretty inefficient due some operational challenges needed to be assessed, attendance issues such as punches or callouts point to the fact that there is a need for improved monitoring and timekeeping procedures. Mostly on route, distance, hour of the day, etc…

* Hr/Route: High mean of 11.242 hours with a standard deviation of 7.37 and a maximum of 26.517 hours, indicating some routes take significantly longer, suggesting potential inefficiencies.  
* Rescues: Mean of 2.596 with a standard deviation of 2.739, showing variability in assistance needed, pointing to operational inconsistencies.  
* Punches: High mean of 10.88 and a standard deviation of 11.17, with a maximum of 42, indicating inconsistent attendance and timekeeping.

#### **Data Preparation** {#data-preparation}

With the purpose of starting the initial statistical analysis of the data, and as a first approach to the data, the project proposes to transform and build a potential net score variable based upon simple aggregation. See Appendix C for a detailed overview of the procedure used with Python Programming Language.    

	Once understanding the contained variables in the weekly dataset and scorecard (See Appendix D), the project proposes to transform the data to a single unit and range (without changing the distribution of the data), in order to account for different scales such as percentages, DPMO, events per hundred trips, or route’s averages. The proposed transformation is Min-Max scaling with the aim to redefine variable’s range from 0 to 1, where 0 is the worst datapoint by a NextGEN’s Delivery Associate in the given week (DA) and 1 is the best datapoint. In this way, it is easier to define a rank between DA, where a weighted scoring model can be applied. Another point to consider is that all variables should be positive predictors to the net score (proposed dependent variable) to account for an easier interpretation of weights –e.g. it is easier to interpret a weight of 30% for variable x1, rather than a variable of –70% for the same variable. Table 1 established the variables and their positive or negative relationship with the proposed overall scoring, where 100% account for a perfect driver. 

|  | *Table 4\.* Positive and Negative Predictors to Response Variable  |  |  |  |  |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **ID** | **Variable** | **Predictor** | **ID** | **Variable** | **Predictor** |
| *1* | *Distraction* | Negative | *10* | *AD* | Positive |
| *2* | *Following* | Negative | *11* | *CDF* | Positive |
| *3* | *Seatbelt* | Negative | *12* | *Tier I* | Negative |
| *4* | *Signal* | Negative | *13* | *HrRoute* | Negative |
| *5* | *Speeding* | Negative | *14* | *Rescues* | Negative |
| *6* | *DCR* | Positive | *15* | *NC/NS* | Negative |
| *7* | *DSB* | Negative | *16* | *Call Out* | Negative |
| *8* | *POD* | Positive | *17* | *Late* | Negative |
| *9* | *CC* | Positive | *18* | *Punches* | Negative |

Therefore, all of the negative predictor variables were inverted by its difference with 1 (max. point) –e.g. if negative predictor variable x1 \= 0.3, as a positive predictor x1 \= 0.7. Secondly, weights were required to continue with the weighted scoring model. Weights for Amazon’s performance indicators –variables 1 through 12– were used and transformed by proportional scaling (sum \= 1.0). Weights for NextGEN’s performance indicators –variables 13 through 18– were obtained by qualitatively interpreting the level of significance to the project sponsor (See also Appendix D), and also transformed by proportional scaling (sum \= 1.0). 

Lastly, the final “Net Score” variable was obtained by assigning the correspondent weights to each variable and applying simple aggregation –also weighting Amazon Score (displayed as “AMZ Score”) by 70%, and NextGEN Score (displayed as “NG Score”) by 30%. It’s important to note that this approach is solely used to include a target variable into the analysis, and the project is taking into account that weights may be subjective and that simple aggregation does not capture complex relationships or interactions between KPIs. See Appendix E to see the results from the Data Preparation Stage. 

#### **Machine Learning Implementation**

Based on the dataset received, a conclusion was stated, for any further analysis there is needed an extended amount of information, any time series analysis can’t be performed since the information given from NEXTGEN is based on a one week analysis due to that basis any prediction based on time cannot be performed.

Cluster analysis the dataset may not have enough diversity or patterns to form any meaningful cluster, since the dataset and its response variables are clearly dependent on each other and clustering analysis relies on variability, a small or short dataset will not capture enough variation of the performance metric. Without a longer time range the clusters would only represent a fixed analysis leading to misinterpretations, you need clear separation in the performance data to create useful groups.

Relating to the PLC, there is no enough time range to observe any process or trend, a short dataset doesn’t allow the identification of distinct phases of a cycle, there is a need of data that can show how a process develops, since the data is from one week, there is not enough information to assess an evolution performance.

## ***Conclusion***

### **Diagnosis of the Current Indicator System**

In order to improve the current performance measurement system, we need to deeply analyze the strengths and the areas of improvement that this system has at the moment. The current performance measurement system has several strengths but also exhibits areas that require improvement. This diagnosis, based on data insights and feedback from the training partner, highlights both dimensions:  
Strengths:

* The system tracks a wide range of KPIs that reflect different operational areas, offering a complete view of performance across various departments.  
* One of the key strengths is its ability to monitor metrics in current time, and this provides up-to-date information to the managers. This supports a quick-decision making process and responding to potential issues.  
* The automation of reports saves a lot of time and reduces the likelihood of human error. This contributes in order to increase efficiency and reliability in the data presentation.  
  Areas of improvement:   
* There is limited focus on customer experience, so introducing more indicators such as customer satisfaction or punctuality could assess those expectations.  
* Lacks external benchmarks, making it difficult to assess relative performance, so providing an industry benchmark would provide a clearer picture of the company’s competition.  
* Expanding efficiency metrics such as fuel consumption or route optimization providing a more evident view of any operational efficiency or sustainability.  
  




| DELIVERABLE 2 |
| :---: |

## ***Statistical Data Analysis*** {#statistical-data-analysis}

###  **Analytical Hierarchical Process** {#analytical-hierarchical-process}

Figure 5 presents the known primary and secondary criteria that were offered by the Project Sponsor –and were decided to be included in the ranking system.   
![][image10]  
***Figure 4\.*** Definition of Criteria for Ranking of Drivers

Table 5 summarizes the known criteria and their respectives weights. Current weights for both AMAZON and NextGEN, and secondary criteria for NextGEN are unavailable. Hence, the project proposes applying AHP methodology to obtain quantitative weights into the criteria structure.   
![][image11]  
***Table 5\.*** Summary of Criteria and Weights Considered for the Ranking of Drivers (incomplete)  
Firstly, a pairwise comparison (Table 6\) was developed for both AMAZON and NextGEN criteria –obtained from the Team Project and consultation sessions with the project sponsor– and concluded an intermediate point between “Most Important (strong)” and “Moderately More Important”.  
![][image12]  
***Table 6\.*** Pairwise Comparison for Criteria in AHP  
Solving for the identity matrix, the resultant eigenvector, concludes with a weight of 80% for AMAZON and 20% for NextGEN.    
![][image13]  
***Table 7\.*** Pairwise Comparison Matrix and Eigenvector for Criteria  
Secondly, a pairwise comparison (Table 6\) was developed for NextGEN’s sub-criteria –obtained from the Team Project and consultation sessions with the project sponsor– and concluded in Table 8\.  
![][image14]  
***Table 8\.*** Pairwise Comparison for Sub-Criteria in AHP  
Solving for the identity matrix, the resultant eigenvector, concludes with weights for each one of the variables included inside the NextGEN criteria.   
![][image15]  
***Table 9\.*** Pairwise Comparison Matrix and Eigenvector for Sub-Criteria  
Updating Table 5, the final weights for each one of the considered sub-criteria for the ranking of the drivers concludes in Table 10\.    
![][image16]  
***Table 10\.*** Summary of Criteria and Weights Considered for the Ranking of Drivers (complete)  
Sorting the variables’ weight in Table 11, it can be concluded that: 

* Key sub-criteria: *Seatbelt*, *Speeding*, *Signal* (related to Safety), *Hours per Route* (related to internal NextGEN operations), *Delivery Completion Rate, Delivery Completion Rate*, and *Customer* *Escalations* (related to quality), have a similar weight distribution ranging from 9.4% to 9.8% and correspond to the Top 7 criteria evaluating drivers.   
* All remaining variables have a weight distribution ranging from 6.3% to 0.6% and is moderately distributed between both Amazon and NextGEN variables. 

![][image17]![][image18]  
***Table 11\.*** Sorted Final Variables’ Weights

### **Ranking of Drivers** {#ranking-of-drivers}

As mentioned in the Deliverable 1, the ranking of drivers –currently developed using Python Programming Language– requires a data preparation stage. Summarized, this consists in:

1. A record for the “ideal driver” is included. This contains the values defined in table 12\. 

| *Table 12\.* Ideal Values Chosen for Ranking |  |  |  |  |  |  |
| ----- | ----- | :---- | :---- | :---- | ----- | :---- |
| **Variable** | **Value** | **Justification** |  | **Variable** | **Value** | **Justification** |
| *Routes* | 1 | Neutral transformation for Step 2 |  | *AD* | 1 | as 100% |
| *Distraction* | 0 |  |  | *CDF* | 1 | as 100% |
| *Following* | 0 |  |  | *Tier I* | 0 |  |
| *Seatbelt* | 0 |  |  | *Hr/Route* | Min | As the fastest possible driving. |
| *Signal* | 0 |  |  | *Rescues* | 0 |  |
| *Speeding* | 0 |  |  | *NC/NS* | 0 |  |
| *DCR* | 1 | as 100% |  | *Call Out* | 0 |  |
| *DSB* | 0 |  |  | *Late* | 0 |  |
| *POD* | 1 | as 100% |  | *Punches* | 0 |  |
| *CC* | 1 | as 100% |  |  |  |  |

2. For NextGEN criteria, the data is transformed into *Events per Routes* (Opportunities), in order to account for the Law of Big Numbers.   
3. MinMax transformation is applied to all data; this maintains the same distribution, where 0 is the worst measure achieved by a driver and 1 is the ideal value.   
4. Negative Predictors are turned into Positive Predictors. For example, a value of *0* in *Seatbelt*, corresponds to zero violations per 100 routes; transformed, a value of *1* in *Seatbelt*, corresponds to 100 seatbelt compliances per 100 routes (best possible record).

Secondly, after the data is prepared, the resulting weights from AHP methodology are applied by simple aggregation. The resulting value is returned as “*Net Score*”, where a 100% corresponds to a driver obtaining the ideal record (described in Table 12\) and a 0% corresponds to a driver obtaining the worst measure in all criteria. Thus, the resulting ranking for drivers for Week 13 is described in Table 13\. 

## ****[figure removed for driver privacy -- showed individually identifiable driver names/IDs]****

***Table 13\.*** Ranking Results for Week 13

*Note*: Response variable “*NextGEN*” uses all decimal digits for sorting.  
In order to validate the results, Figures 5, 6, and 7 were developed. 

| *Figure 5\.* Performance of the Best Driver during Week 13\. ![][image20] *Note*: Data corresponding to driver the #1-ranked driver.    | *Figure 6\.* Performance of the Median Driver during Week 13\. ![][image21] *Note*: Median driver corresponds to the driver ranked 26th out of 52 drivers. Data corresponding to drive the median-ranked (26th) driver.  |
| ----- | ----- |
| ***Figure 7\.*** Performance of the Worst Driver during Week 13\. ![][image22] *Note*: Data corresponding to driver the #52 (lowest-ranked) driver. | ***Figure 8\.*** Legend for Performance Graphs ![][image23] |

On the whole, Figure 5 through 7 allow us to visually compare the individual rankings for each one of the variables (line plot); whereas, labels exemplify the non-transformed values (ideal, worst, and actual from the driver). The best driver (Figure 6\) has the highest –if not a high– ranking in all variables; the median (26th) driver (Figure 7\) has moderately high performance, except for two key criteria –*Hr/Route* and *DSB*; the worst driver (Figure 8\) has the lowest performance overall, accounting for two of the worst achievable data points –*Tier I* and *CC*– and low ranking in key criteria –*Hr/Route, DCR, DSB,* and *Tier I*.   
	To sum up, best performances by drivers contain exclusively best scores and moderately high scores –excluding driver 6th (the 6th-ranked driver) who achieved the worst record for *Call Out* (third-to-last critical variable). On the other hand, as driver performance decreases, lower or even the lowest records for variables are present in the heat map –meaning those responsible for underperformance, are correctly positioned in the lowest positions in the ranking.  

#### **Use of New Variables** {#use-of-new-variables}

Evidently, the new proposed variable ‘*Net Score*’ aids the training partner with aims to rank the drivers based upon the collected data from both AMAZON and NextGEN criteria. It is composed of all of the available variables that are of vital importance for both sponsors, AMAZON and NextGEN CEO. It acts in accordance with the given factors of AMAZON Delivery Associate’s Scorecard –directly impacting the company’s revenue. On the other hand, the obtained coefficients derived from the AHP methodology, naturally bond with the Amazon variables and respective weights. Likewise, those same final weights may be in assistance if a solely *Amazon Score* or *NextGen Score* is in need to be measured. To sum up, the obtained variable of ‘Net Score’ perfectly describes the behavior of drivers in a synthesized manner and  is the best proposal of the project in order to comply with its objective. 

#### **Summary of Conclusions** {#summary-of-conclusions}

The statistical analysis of driver performance using the Analytical Hierarchical Process (AHP) provides a structured method for ranking drivers based on key criteria, and it offers valuable insights that are critical for designing a meaningful and actionable dashboard.

The AHP methodology was applied to establish a weighted hierarchy of criteria essential for evaluating driver performance. The analysis focuses on two main driver groups, AMAZON and NextGEN, each with specific sub-criteria tailored to their operational demands. The AHP results indicate that AMAZON criteria hold 80% of the overall importance, emphasizing safety and delivery quality metrics, such as Seatbelt, Speeding, and Signal compliance. NextGEN criteria, with a 20% weight, prioritize factors related to attendance and time efficiency, including Hours per Route, Rescues, and No Call/No Show occurrences.

Among these, certain key sub-criteria—Seatbelt, Speeding, Signal, Hours per Route, Delivery Completion Rate (DCR), Delivery Success Behavior (DSB), and Tier Infractions—stand out as the most heavily weighted factors. These metrics are, therefore, critical in assessing drivers’ adherence to safety, efficiency, and delivery quality standards.

To facilitate performance comparison, an “ideal driver” model was developed, establishing a benchmark with optimal values. This ideal profile is defined by zero violations for safety-related criteria, minimal time for route completion, and 100% compliance in quality metrics. Through a process of score transformation, negative predictors—such as undesirable behaviors (e.g., speeding and missed punches)—were standardized as positive metrics, with scores normalized on a scale from 0 (worst) to 1 (ideal).

Each driver’s performance is measured against this ideal, producing a “Net Score” that allows for straightforward ranking. For instance, in Week 13, the best-performing driver scored 97.6%, while the lowest score was 73%. This range provides a snapshot of current performance and highlights areas where improvement is needed, either through training or operational adjustments.

The AHP-derived scores for individual drivers allow us to analyze performance across different levels: best, median, and worst performers. The best-performing driver, with a Net Score of 97.6%, exhibits near-ideal compliance in critical metrics like Seatbelt, Speeding, and Signal compliance, as well as Hours per Route. Minor deviations may exist in some less heavily weighted criteria, but overall, this driver is close to the ideal.

In contrast, the median driver, scoring 90.2%, displays good performance but shows noticeable gaps in criteria such as DCR, DSB, and Rescues, suggesting opportunities for improvement, especially in delivery-related areas. Finally, the worst-performing driver, with a score of 73.0%, shows significant deviations in multiple areas, particularly in time efficiency (Hours per Route) and quality compliance (DCR, DSB, Tier Infractions). This profile suggests that targeted interventions, such as additional training or operational support, may be necessary.

## ***Development of the Dashboard*** {#development-of-the-dashboard}

After analyzing driver performance using the Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP), we calculated a Net Score for each driver. This score allowed us to see who’s excelling and who might need additional support, giving us a clear view of performance across key areas.

To bring these insights to life and make the data easy to explore, we created a dashboard that offers a visual breakdown of driver performance. This way, the company can quickly see patterns, spot trends, and understand how drivers are performing overall. This serves as a practical tool that highlights strengths, shows potential areas for growth and helps the people involved make informed decisions to support and guide their team more effectively.

The dashboard first has a section called "Driver Net Score" which is focused on having easy access to a driver’s overall performance. Here, the person in charge can simply enter the Driver ID, and the dashboard instantly displays the driver’s name and Net Score. This approach allows managers to quickly check a driver’s overall performance without having to search through the entire dataset. With just a few clicks, managers get a general view of a driver’s effectiveness, offering a general performance metric without getting into the details of each metric. This makes it especially useful for quick check-ins or when high-level insights are all that’s needed.

*[figure removed for driver privacy -- showed individually identifiable driver names/IDs]*  
***Figure 10\.*** Driver Net Score

The dashboard also has an interactive line chart that shows each driver’s performance across all the key variables that we’re evaluating. It’s a straightforward way to see, at a glance, how well a driver is doing in each area, with values ranging from 0 to 1, where 1 represents the best possible score and 0 the lowest. This setup gives managers a quick, clear view of how each driver is performing in different areas, helping them to see not only each driver’s strengths and weaknesses but also how each variable contrasts against the others.

We also implemented a slicer, where you can choose to look at just one driver or select multiple drivers to compare them side-by-side. This makes it easy for managers to see how different drivers measure up across each variable, helping them spot trends, identify standout performers, or catch areas where someone might need support. By offering this visual and flexible view, the dashboard turns data into practical insights that help the company target efforts to improve their performance.

*[figure removed for driver privacy -- showed individually identifiable driver names/IDs]*  
***Figure 11\.*** Line Chart of performance in each variable by driver with slider

Another feature we added to the dashboard is a set of bar charts highlighting drivers with the highest and lowest Net Scores. In Figure 13, a bar chart shows the top-performing drivers, allowing managers to see their scores and compare them to the next two best drivers. Similarly, Figure 14 displays the drivers with the lowest Net Scores, helping identify those who may need additional support and comparing their scores with the two other lowest-scoring drivers.

These visualizations make it easy to identify standout performers and those who might benefit from coaching, providing quick insights at both ends of the performance spectrum.

*[figure removed for driver privacy -- showed individually identifiable driver names/IDs]**[figure removed for driver privacy -- showed individually identifiable driver names/IDs]*  
***Figure 12\.*** Bar Graph of the 3 top drivers             ***Figure 13\.*** Bar Graph of the 3 worse drivers

## 

| DELIVERABLE 3 |
| :---: |

## ***New Statistical Analysis*** {#new-statistical-analysis}

**Cluster Analysis**  
Now that the development of the ranking of the drivers was defined, a cluster analysis will be implemented in order to have some statistical insights on why some of the drivers develop some good rankings and others not; the variables used in the clustering process reflect essential aspects of operational compliance, efficiency, and overall performance. By understanding the behavior of each cluster, actionable insights can be derived to refine strategies and improve outcomes.  
![][image28]  
Remember, all variables are already standardized, 0 meaning the worst grading and 1 meaning the best grading. Cluster 1: (3), Cluster 2: (8), Cluster 3: (41)

***Cluster 1***  
Insights: We will focus at first on cluster 1 (Net Score: 0.779): This cluster represents the worst rankings of the drivers. Cluster 1 represents a small group of drivers, likely a niche group characterized by their high compliance in safety-related metrics, despite their strong relationship to KPIs such as "Following" (1.00), "Seatbelt" (0.968), "Signal" (1.00) suggesting that Cluster 1 drivers can adhere to rules and regulations, minimizing risk. Their low "POD" (0.442) and "CDF" (0.460) scores highlight inefficiencies in delivery completion and documentation. The "CC" (0.000) suggests either a lack of customer engagement or unresolved complaints.  
Actions: Provide targeted training for drivers that can relate to this cluster to improve their delivery documentation and customer interactions, investigate whether the operational environment or some tasks assigned to these drivers are contributing to these inefficiencies. Cluster 1’s small size indicates a specialized group. Addressing their specific inefficiencies in delivery processes can help improve their overall contribution to the organization.

## ***![][image29]***

***Cluster 2***  
Insights: The next cluster created, represents a moderate-sized group of drivers that performs exceptionally well in efficiency metrics like "Tier I Tasks" (1.00) and "DCR" (0.929),  showing ability to prioritize and complete key deliveries. This suggests that Cluster 2 drivers are adept at handling high-priority and time-sensitive tasks. Strong scores in "NC/NS" (0.906) indicate their ability to minimize errors and service breaches, maintaining reliability. Despite their efficiency, their lateness is a significant weakness, with a poor "Late" (0.249) score indicating frequent delivery delays. This may be caused by poor scheduling or overloaded routes. Additionally, their low "DSB" (0.554) suggests they often fail to meet delivery standards, compromising quality to complete deliveries. Also moderate "POD" (0.651) scores reveal room for improvement in documenting deliveries.  
Actions

* \-Reduce Distractions, introduce some mechanisms to eliminate those distractions, such as optimization on task sequences or focus-enhancing policies.  
* \-Deploy drivers in for operations requiring rapid response and prioritization of high-impact deliveries, as they excel at Tier I tasks.  
* \-Provide tools or training to enhance the ability to document deliveries accurately. Digital proof of delivery solutions might help reduce those errors and delays.

By addressing distractions and improving delivery documentation on cluster 2, and since they excel in efficiency and reliability, they can become Next Gen’s group for excellence.

.***![][image30]***

***Cluster 3***  
Insights: The largest group. Their high scores in "Call Out" (0.948) highlight their reliability in handling emergencies and urgent tasks, achieving an excellent "Late" score (0.952), indicating consistent on-time deliveries making them the most punctual group and a key driver of customer satisfaction, but "DSB" (0.835) and "POD" (0.769) can be seen that there is good relationship with delivery standards and documentation but leave room for improvement. While "Rescues" (0.805) is decent, it’s not great, indicating over-reliance on their size to handle disruptions rather than operational flexibility.  
Actions

* \-Focus on improving "DSB" and "POD" with audits and simplified delivery procedures.  
* \-Invest in tools for real-time delivery tracking and proof-of-delivery systems.  
* \-Use the "Rescues" (0.805) score as a baseline to refine processes for emergency handling. \-Improve resource allocation and contingency planning to make this group more agile.

![][image31]  
A mix of operational strengths and weaknesses. Cluster 1 demonstrates exceptional safety compliance but underperforms in delivery fulfillment and customer engagement, requiring improvements in these areas to become more balanced. Cluster 2 excels in efficiency, completing tasks effectively, but struggles with frequent lateness and delivery standard adherence, indicating the need to balance speed with quality. Cluster 3, being the largest group, excels in punctuality but requires better delivery standards, documentation practices, and safety compliance to maximize its overall impact on organizational performance. Addressing these specific challenges while promoting best practices will enhance operational success.

## ***Value Propositions*** {#value-propositions}

Finally, the statistical analysis that was developed throughout this project can highlight some key areas in which NextGEN can enhance some of its operations mainly with data-driven strategies specific improvement opportunities have been identified that can increase driver performance, reduce operational costs, and boost customer satisfaction. The next value propositions are designed to build upon these insights:

* Dashboard with real-time Operational insights:  
  * A streamlined, visually engaging dashboard will deliver real-time updates alongside historical comparisons of Key Performance Indicators (KPIs). This tool empowers managers with actionable insights, enabling them to proactively monitor driver performance, address issues as they arise, and maintain alignment with operational objectives. The dashboard serves as a central hub for informed decision-making and continuous improvement.  
* Performance coaching and Rewards program:  
  * NextGEN can provide a more enhanced coaching program to address the behaviors of other drivers who may need some guidance from high performing drivers. This initiative promotes adequacy and uniformity in skill training, whereas a system of award and incentives provides the hall for the most proficient performers so that other drivers would also strive for, and maintain higher levels of performance.  
* Adaptive metrics for strategic alignment:  
  * To ensure sustained relevance, the KPIs underpinning the driver ranking system will be regularly reviewed and adjusted. This process ensures that the metrics accurately reflect current business priorities and operational realities, allowing the system to adapt seamlessly to organizational shifts while maintaining its effectiveness as a performance evaluation tool.  
* Data-Driven Driver deployment:  
  * When using information from the driver performance rankings, NextGEN can effectively schedule and assign the routes. In high priority deliveries the best employees should be given the job during training and development, under performing personnel receive training in aspects that are useful to the organization. It also enables the workforce to be used most effectively and in doing so, results in improved service delivery.  
* Personalized Growth sessions:  
  * The driver feedback meetings will be regular and will offer to each driver rankings, KPI data, as well as the historical analysis of all careers. These sessions help to provide an opportunity to freely talk about performance, successes, and missed calls. This keeps the driver on a clear actionable plan of what they should and should not do in order to achieve and maintain a successful run.

  ## ***Conclusions*** {#conclusions}

In the final phase of this project, we enhanced our analysis by integrating an additional statistical method, clusters; alongside the tools previously used, such as the Analytical Hierarchical Process (AHP) and multicriteria analysis. This allowed us to gain deeper insights beyond the driver rankings, further improving our understanding of their performance.

Through the combination of these analyses and the development of a comprehensive dashboard, we were able to assess driver performance based on various metrics. Not only did we obtain the final performance number, but we also visualized how these metrics were behaving to explain the overall performance, providing a clearer picture of the strengths and areas for improvement of each driver.

Finally, what we did was group drivers into clusters based on their performance patterns, so that NextGEN Deliveries can implement more targeted interventions, ensuring that each driver receives the necessary support to address their specific weaknesses. This approach will help performance improvements to be more efficient. The insights from this analysis suggest that by addressing specific metrics that drivers weren’t  performing as well as others, and fostering a culture of continuous improvement, NextGEN Deliveries can enhance operational efficiency, increase customer satisfaction and reduce costs. Additionally, strategically allocating drivers to roles that target their strengths, coupled with mentorship and incentive programs, will promote sustained performance consistency. These data driven strategies will help turn valuable insights into actionable plans that drive long-term success and operational excellence.

## ***Individual Learnings*** {#individual-learnings}

***Luis Eduardo Ibarra Zepeda***: By conducting statistical analysis throughout following the requirements needed to develop the main objective of the project, which is having a concise and logical way to rank the drivers of Next Gen, personally, the hardest part was the fact that the dataset given by the training partner is to small to have good insights, since some distributions, trends won’t be shown that easily with such a small dataset, so finding actual procedures of value with the usage of first of all descriptive statistics, actually asking and having bigger context on what does each variable mean and the importance the company gives to each variable to have a preciser insight on how this variables actually reflects on the drivers performance, with having that information, it is a lot easier to make any statistical analysis, such as the weights given to each variable, and how each variable can relate to each other.  
***Adolfo Fernando Teliz Colchado***: Data Science Projects is truly a multidisciplinary field that may require various fields of knowledge. In order to develop the project, the team needed to fully familiarize with Business Understanding, Data Understanding, Data Preparation, Modeling and Evaluation methodologies and stages. Likewise, various software for both Data Manipulation and Visualization were used during the project such as: MS Excel, Minitab Statistical Software, Jamovi, and Python Programming Language. The complexity of data science projects require a holistic view from both data scientists, analysts, and shareholders. Finally, Machine Learning tools assisted the project to gather valuable insights invisible to the usual statistical analysis or data visualizations. For further industrial engineering projects, the creative, accurate and ethical use of these tools is vital to create a culture of Data-Driven Decision Making and Continuous Improvement in a company’s operations.   
***Kenneth Wünsch:***  I learned how to use the AHP for structured decision-making. Defining and weighting criteria taught me to prioritize based on impact, while pairwise comparisons provided nuanced insights for accurate rankings. Data normalization, particularly with MinMax scaling, showed me how to ensure fair, objective comparisons. I also saw the power of visual tools like heatmaps to clearly communicate performance patterns and variability. Analyzing top, median, and low-ranking drivers helped me understand how individual criteria affect overall results and highlighted areas for improvement. This project strengthened my skills in balancing subjective and objective analysis, applying structured methods for clear, data-driven decision-making.  
***Mirari Ferrer Etcheverry:*** This project provided a valuable opportunity to apply the machine learning tools we studied in class to a real-world challenge. It required us to evaluate which tools and methods were most relevant to the problem, while filtering out less applicable approaches. After carefully considering the best way to address the problem, we achieved good results in addressing the challenge, finding effective driver rankings. Through this experience, I was not only able to solve a practical problem but also gained the ability to analyze the performance in real time; identifying areas for improvement and understanding the factors that cause these outcomes; gaining  insights and monitoring changes was for refining strategies for performance improvement. This challenge was a great experience that allowed me to apply classroom knowledge to real-life scenarios, while developing my problem solving skills and learning how to make decisions based on my own criteria.   
***Erick Oseguera Salinas:*** In this final deliverable, I focused more on tools and methodologies that played a huge role in achieving the goals of our challenge. The most satisfying work done to enhance the dashboard was the time spent on it. I found it interesting how even with complex and organized data, the right combination of visualization enhances numerous decision-making procedures. Another problem was in designing a working interface for the dashboard taking into account that the end-user may not have coding or technical skills (since this would also be accessible to the drivers). This meant that to stay useful and employed it needed to be complex enough to be useful to a power user but simple enough that anyone could pick it up and find utility in it. I also get a better understanding of data normalization methods like MinMax scaling where all features are normalized in a way that they all contribute to performance measures. This process has emphasized the importance of data pre-processing and cleaning to the quality of the analysis that is conducted. In a personal and professional point of view, this project was a great help for improving my technical skill in data processing, visualization, and normalization. These ones are not restricted to data science but are also useful in business analysis and any other sector that involves extensive data analysis. However, this experience also illustrated the importance of data quality; low-quality data can dramatically affect the quality of the final result. In this way, I deepened my understanding of the combination of technical approach and real-world applicability which will undoubtedly be useful in subsequent work in the generation of value through data analytics.
